answer:
**Diwan-E-Zauq** by Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, chapter 56 ("hungaama garm hasti-e-na-paaidaar ka").[^1]

This ghazal's every couplet ends with the refrain "is love," matching the radif '-aar ka' in the original Urdu.[^1] The chinar-on-the-grave couplet is:

> My heart's own fire, in the grave, will glow, is love.  
> A tall Chinar upon my tomb will grow, is love.[^2]

The bittersweet-teardrops couplet, referring to their juice as that of "Eden's pomegranate" (rendering *khuld-e-bareen*, the pomegranate of the highest paradise), is:

> You ask what sweetness bitter teardrops show, is love?  
> The juice of Eden's pomegranate in its flow, is love.[^3]

The ghazal closes with the poet's *takhallus* (signature): "O Zauq, if you are wise, then from this world just go, is love. / This tavern has no place for those who think they know, is love."[^1]

[^1]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, [chapter 56](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/56-hungaama-garm-hasti-e-na-paaidaar-ka/translation/).

[^2]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, [paragraph](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/56-hungaama-garm-hasti-e-na-paaidaar-ka/translation/#p-f77039).

[^3]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, [paragraph](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/56-hungaama-garm-hasti-e-na-paaidaar-ka/translation/#p-a4f044).
